title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 02

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Angkor, c. 1200: “The ruler has restored the great reservoir and assigned officials to measure water for the fields. Rice from royal lands shall support the temple, the scribes, and the workers who maintain the embankments.” What was the most likely purpose of this inscription?

choices.A: To encourage merchants to abandon inland trade routes
choices.B: To announce the end of religious authority in Southeast Asia
choices.C: To present the ruler as a legitimate provider of order, fertility, and religious merit
choices.D: To promote democratic participation in village government

questions.correctAnswerText: To present the ruler as a legitimate provider of order, fertility, and religious merit
questions.explanation: The inscription links royal authority to irrigation, temple support, officials, and agricultural order.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Angkor, c. 1200: “The ruler has restored the great reservoir and assigned officials to measure water for the fields. Rice from royal lands shall support the temple, the scribes, and the workers who maintain the embankments.” Which environmental challenge is most directly addressed in the inscription?

choices.A: Managing water for wet-rice agriculture in a monsoon climate
choices.B: Adapting to Arctic sea-ice conditions
choices.C: Preventing overgrazing by nomadic herders
choices.D: Reclaiming desert land for date-palm farming

questions.correctAnswerText: Managing water for wet-rice agriculture in a monsoon climate
questions.explanation: The reservoir, fields, and embankments show state efforts to manage water for rice cultivation.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Angkor, c. 1200: “The ruler has restored the great reservoir and assigned officials to measure water for the fields. Rice from royal lands shall support the temple, the scribes, and the workers who maintain the embankments.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religion and state power were connected?

choices.A: “Assigned officials”
choices.B: “Measure water”
choices.C: “For the fields”
choices.D: “Rice from royal lands shall support the temple”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Rice from royal lands shall support the temple”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects royal resources and religious institutions.

questions.prompt: Originalized temple inscription, Angkor, c. 1200: “The ruler has restored the great reservoir and assigned officials to measure water for the fields. Rice from royal lands shall support the temple, the scribes, and the workers who maintain the embankments.” Which material feature would best illustrate the type of state power described?

choices.A: A steam-powered textile mill
choices.B: A large temple complex connected to reservoirs and canals
choices.C: A printed nationalist newspaper
choices.D: A container port with automated cranes

questions.correctAnswerText: A large temple complex connected to reservoirs and canals
questions.explanation: Angkor’s state power was closely tied to monumental architecture and water-management systems.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account, Central Asia, c. 1250: “With a tablet of authority, a messenger may obtain horses at relay stations and pass safely across the khan’s roads. Merchants say that guards punish bandits, and caravans now carry silk, medicines, and metal goods across lands that were once dangerous.” Which development is best reflected in the account?

choices.A: Mongol rule facilitated commerce across parts of Eurasia.
choices.B: Maritime trade replaced all overland exchange.
choices.C: Central Asian cities were cut off from long-distance trade.
choices.D: The Silk Roads declined because rulers refused to protect merchants.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mongol rule facilitated commerce across parts of Eurasia.
questions.explanation: The account describes protected roads, relay stations, and goods moving across Mongol-controlled territory.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account, Central Asia, c. 1250: “With a tablet of authority, a messenger may obtain horses at relay stations and pass safely across the khan’s roads. Merchants say that guards punish bandits, and caravans now carry silk, medicines, and metal goods across lands that were once dangerous.” Which audience would most likely have found this account useful?

choices.A: Monks seeking to end all contact with merchants
choices.B: Peasants attempting to avoid taxation by local landlords
choices.C: Merchants or envoys planning travel through Mongol-controlled territories
choices.D: Factory owners looking for wage laborers

questions.correctAnswerText: Merchants or envoys planning travel through Mongol-controlled territories
questions.explanation: Details about tablets, relay stations, horses, and safety would be useful to travelers and merchants.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account, Central Asia, c. 1250: “With a tablet of authority, a messenger may obtain horses at relay stations and pass safely across the khan’s roads. Merchants say that guards punish bandits, and caravans now carry silk, medicines, and metal goods across lands that were once dangerous.” Which phrase most directly supports the claim that imperial infrastructure encouraged exchange?

choices.A: “Silk, medicines, and metal goods”
choices.B: “Relay stations”
choices.C: “Once dangerous”
choices.D: “Across lands”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Relay stations”
questions.explanation: Relay stations are a direct example of infrastructure that supported movement and exchange.

questions.prompt: Originalized travel account, Central Asia, c. 1250: “With a tablet of authority, a messenger may obtain horses at relay stations and pass safely across the khan’s roads. Merchants say that guards punish bandits, and caravans now carry silk, medicines, and metal goods across lands that were once dangerous.” Which unintended consequence was most associated with the increased movement described?

choices.A: The immediate end of pastoral nomadism
choices.B: The disappearance of caravan cities
choices.C: The collapse of all disease transmission across Eurasia
choices.D: The spread of epidemic diseases along exchange networks

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of epidemic diseases along exchange networks
questions.explanation: Increased cross-regional movement helped spread disease, including the Black Death, across trade routes.

questions.prompt: Described court painting, Mughal Empire, early seventeenth century: “The emperor sits beneath a jeweled canopy. Around him stand nobles ranked by closeness to the throne, including Rajput commanders, Persianate scribes, and military officers. A clerk records gifts and appointments.” Which interpretation is best supported by the visual description?

choices.A: The Mughal state rejected court hierarchy.
choices.B: The Mughal emperor used ceremonial rank and diverse elites to organize imperial authority.
choices.C: Mughal rule depended entirely on elected village assemblies.
choices.D: The Mughal court excluded non-Muslim elites from all imperial service.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Mughal emperor used ceremonial rank and diverse elites to organize imperial authority.
questions.explanation: The painting shows ranked nobles, imperial ceremony, and diverse elite service around the emperor.

questions.prompt: Described court painting, Mughal Empire, early seventeenth century: “The emperor sits beneath a jeweled canopy. Around him stand nobles ranked by closeness to the throne, including Rajput commanders, Persianate scribes, and military officers. A clerk records gifts and appointments.” The inclusion of Rajput commanders most directly illustrates which feature of Mughal rule?

choices.A: The complete separation of military service from social status
choices.B: The replacement of imperial elites by European merchants
choices.C: The abolition of aristocratic ranking
choices.D: The incorporation of local and regional elites into imperial administration

questions.correctAnswerText: The incorporation of local and regional elites into imperial administration
questions.explanation: Rajput commanders served within the Mughal system, showing elite incorporation.

questions.prompt: Described court painting, Mughal Empire, early seventeenth century: “The emperor sits beneath a jeweled canopy. Around him stand nobles ranked by closeness to the throne, including Rajput commanders, Persianate scribes, and military officers. A clerk records gifts and appointments.” Which broader cultural context best helps explain the court scene?

choices.A: Mughal rulers blended Persianate court culture with South Asian political traditions.
choices.B: Mughal rulers rejected all written record keeping.
choices.C: South Asia had no contact with Islamic cultural traditions before 1750.
choices.D: Mughal legitimacy depended only on maritime trade.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mughal rulers blended Persianate court culture with South Asian political traditions.
questions.explanation: Persianate scribes, Rajput commanders, and Mughal court ceremony show cultural and political synthesis.

questions.prompt: Described court painting, Mughal Empire, early seventeenth century: “The emperor sits beneath a jeweled canopy. Around him stand nobles ranked by closeness to the throne, including Rajput commanders, Persianate scribes, and military officers. A clerk records gifts and appointments.” Which evidence would best support an argument that Mughal authority depended on bureaucratic management as well as military power?

choices.A: The jeweled canopy over the emperor
choices.B: The nobles standing near the throne
choices.C: The clerk recording gifts and appointments
choices.D: The presence of military officers

questions.correctAnswerText: The clerk recording gifts and appointments
questions.explanation: Record keeping indicates bureaucratic management beyond military power.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tokugawa edict, Japan, 1630s: “No missionary shall enter the realm. Ships from abroad may trade only under inspection at the appointed port. Local lords must report hidden converts and unauthorized voyages, for disorder comes when foreign teachings disturb loyalty.” What was the most likely purpose of this edict?

choices.A: To encourage unrestricted European settlement throughout Japan
choices.B: To regulate foreign contact and limit the political influence of Christianity
choices.C: To abolish all domestic authority of Japanese lords
choices.D: To make Japan a colony of the Dutch East India Company

questions.correctAnswerText: To regulate foreign contact and limit the political influence of Christianity
questions.explanation: The edict restricts missionaries, foreign ships, converts, and unauthorized voyages.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tokugawa edict, Japan, 1630s: “No missionary shall enter the realm. Ships from abroad may trade only under inspection at the appointed port. Local lords must report hidden converts and unauthorized voyages, for disorder comes when foreign teachings disturb loyalty.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religious concerns shaped Japanese policy?

choices.A: “Ships from abroad”
choices.B: “Foreign teachings disturb loyalty”
choices.C: “The appointed port”
choices.D: “Local lords”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Foreign teachings disturb loyalty”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows concern that Christianity could undermine political loyalty.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tokugawa edict, Japan, 1630s: “No missionary shall enter the realm. Ships from abroad may trade only under inspection at the appointed port. Local lords must report hidden converts and unauthorized voyages, for disorder comes when foreign teachings disturb loyalty.” Which broader context best explains why the Tokugawa state issued such regulations?

choices.A: The collapse of all maritime trade in East Asia
choices.B: The rise of transoceanic European missionary and commercial activity in Asia
choices.C: The spread of industrial railroads across Japan
choices.D: The end of Chinese influence in East Asian commerce before 1200

questions.correctAnswerText: The rise of transoceanic European missionary and commercial activity in Asia
questions.explanation: Japanese restrictions responded to increased European maritime trade and missionary presence.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tokugawa edict, Japan, 1630s: “No missionary shall enter the realm. Ships from abroad may trade only under inspection at the appointed port. Local lords must report hidden converts and unauthorized voyages, for disorder comes when foreign teachings disturb loyalty.” The edict is best understood as an example of which broader process?

choices.A: A state response to new global maritime connections
choices.B: The formation of a democratic Atlantic republic
choices.C: A nomadic conquest of settled agricultural land
choices.D: The creation of a twentieth-century military alliance

questions.correctAnswerText: A state response to new global maritime connections
questions.explanation: The Tokugawa state regulated foreign contact produced by expanding oceanic networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by a South American creole revolutionary, c. 1815: “We were taught to obey distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns. If sovereignty belongs to the people, then Americans cannot remain children under a king across the sea.” Which development most directly shaped the ideas in the letter?

choices.A: Enlightenment and Atlantic revolutionary ideas about popular sovereignty
choices.B: The spread of fascism in interwar Europe
choices.C: The creation of the Mongol postal system
choices.D: The rise of container shipping

questions.correctAnswerText: Enlightenment and Atlantic revolutionary ideas about popular sovereignty
questions.explanation: The letter uses sovereignty and anti-monarchical language associated with Atlantic revolutions.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by a South American creole revolutionary, c. 1815: “We were taught to obey distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns. If sovereignty belongs to the people, then Americans cannot remain children under a king across the sea.” Which phrase best supports the author’s criticism of colonial rule?

choices.A: “Sovereignty belongs”
choices.B: “The people”
choices.C: “Americans”
choices.D: “Distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns”
questions.explanation: This phrase criticizes rule by imperial officials unfamiliar with local conditions.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by a South American creole revolutionary, c. 1815: “We were taught to obey distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns. If sovereignty belongs to the people, then Americans cannot remain children under a king across the sea.” The letter most directly reflects which broader ideological shift?

choices.A: The rejection of nationalism in favor of dynastic empire
choices.B: The spread of political language that challenged hereditary monarchy and imperial rule
choices.C: The return of feudal obligations as the basis of citizenship
choices.D: The defense of absolute monarchy as a universal right

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of political language that challenged hereditary monarchy and imperial rule
questions.explanation: The source uses popular sovereignty to challenge distant royal authority.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by a South American creole revolutionary, c. 1815: “We were taught to obey distant ministers who know neither our roads nor our towns. If sovereignty belongs to the people, then Americans cannot remain children under a king across the sea.” Which statement best evaluates the author’s argument?

choices.A: The author rejects independence because colonial officials understand local conditions.
choices.B: The author defends empire by claiming monarchy protects American liberties.
choices.C: The author uses popular sovereignty to argue against rule by distant imperial officials.
choices.D: The author argues that social hierarchy should be abolished immediately in all colonies.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author uses popular sovereignty to argue against rule by distant imperial officials.
questions.explanation: The author argues that sovereignty belongs to the people rather than a king across the sea.

questions.prompt:
Table: Egyptian cotton exports and foreign debt, approximate trends
Period | Cotton exports | Foreign debt
1840s | Moderate | Low
1860s | High during global cotton shortage | Rising
1880s | High export dependence | Heavy foreign control over finances
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Egypt’s cotton economy became connected to global industrial demand and foreign financial influence.
choices.B: Egypt avoided all involvement in nineteenth-century global capitalism.
choices.C: Cotton exports declined as industrialization spread.
choices.D: Foreign debt disappeared as export dependence increased.

questions.correctAnswerText: Egypt’s cotton economy became connected to global industrial demand and foreign financial influence.
questions.explanation: The table links high cotton exports to rising debt and foreign financial control.

questions.prompt:
Table: Egyptian cotton exports and foreign debt, approximate trends
Period | Cotton exports | Foreign debt
1840s | Moderate | Low
1860s | High during global cotton shortage | Rising
1880s | High export dependence | Heavy foreign control over finances
Which evidence most directly supports the claim that economic dependence could weaken political autonomy?

choices.A: “Moderate”
choices.B: “Low”
choices.C: “High during global cotton shortage”
choices.D: “Heavy foreign control over finances”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Heavy foreign control over finances”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows how economic dependence affected political autonomy.

questions.prompt:
Table: Egyptian cotton exports and foreign debt, approximate trends
Period | Cotton exports | Foreign debt
1840s | Moderate | Low
1860s | High during global cotton shortage | Rising
1880s | High export dependence | Heavy foreign control over finances
Which broader environmental and economic context best explains the expansion of cotton cultivation?

choices.A: The end of all irrigated agriculture in the Nile valley
choices.B: The decline of textile manufacturing in industrial economies
choices.C: The replacement of cash crops by subsistence farming
choices.D: The use of agricultural land and irrigation systems to supply industrial textile markets

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of agricultural land and irrigation systems to supply industrial textile markets
questions.explanation: Cotton cultivation expanded to supply industrial textile production, reshaping land and irrigation use.

questions.prompt:
Table: Egyptian cotton exports and foreign debt, approximate trends
Period | Cotton exports | Foreign debt
1840s | Moderate | Low
1860s | High during global cotton shortage | Rising
1880s | High export dependence | Heavy foreign control over finances
Which evidence would best support an argument that transportation technology intensified Egypt’s integration into global markets?

choices.A: A record showing lower demand for cotton cloth
choices.B: Steamship and canal traffic records showing faster movement of cotton to European mills
choices.C: A decree banning all exports from Egyptian ports
choices.D: A religious text criticizing foreign merchants

questions.correctAnswerText: Steamship and canal traffic records showing faster movement of cotton to European mills
questions.explanation: Steamships and canals accelerated export flows and tied Egypt more tightly to industrial markets.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, early 1930s: “A frightened voter stands between two doors. One door is labeled ‘parliamentary debate’ and appears cracked and slow to open. The other door is labeled ‘order now’ and is guarded by a uniformed leader holding a baton. Behind the voter, a breadline stretches down the street.” Which interpretation is best supported by the cartoon?

choices.A: Economic crisis made authoritarian promises of order more appealing to some voters.
choices.B: Parliamentary systems became more stable during the Great Depression.
choices.C: Fascist movements rejected all forms of mass politics.
choices.D: Economic hardship had no connection to political radicalization.

questions.correctAnswerText: Economic crisis made authoritarian promises of order more appealing to some voters.
questions.explanation: Breadlines and the promise of “order now” show how economic crisis could strengthen authoritarian movements.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, early 1930s: “A frightened voter stands between two doors. One door is labeled ‘parliamentary debate’ and appears cracked and slow to open. The other door is labeled ‘order now’ and is guarded by a uniformed leader holding a baton. Behind the voter, a breadline stretches down the street.” Which broader context best explains the cartoon?

choices.A: The prosperity created by postwar decolonization
choices.B: The Great Depression and widespread doubts about liberal democratic governments
choices.C: The immediate success of the League of Nations in preventing economic collapse
choices.D: The decline of nationalism after World War I

questions.correctAnswerText: The Great Depression and widespread doubts about liberal democratic governments
questions.explanation: The breadline and weakened parliamentary door reflect Great Depression-era instability and political disillusionment.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, early 1930s: “A frightened voter stands between two doors. One door is labeled ‘parliamentary debate’ and appears cracked and slow to open. The other door is labeled ‘order now’ and is guarded by a uniformed leader holding a baton. Behind the voter, a breadline stretches down the street.” Which detail most directly suggests criticism of authoritarian politics?

choices.A: The uniformed leader holds a baton while promising “order now.”
choices.B: The breadline stretches down the street.
choices.C: The voter appears frightened.
choices.D: The parliamentary door is cracked.

questions.correctAnswerText: The uniformed leader holds a baton while promising “order now.”
questions.explanation: The baton suggests coercion behind authoritarian promises of order.

questions.prompt: Described political cartoon, Europe, early 1930s: “A frightened voter stands between two doors. One door is labeled ‘parliamentary debate’ and appears cracked and slow to open. The other door is labeled ‘order now’ and is guarded by a uniformed leader holding a baton. Behind the voter, a breadline stretches down the street.” Which later development was most directly connected to the political trend criticized in the cartoon?

choices.A: The spread of nonviolent decolonization movements only
choices.B: The end of militarism in Europe
choices.C: The expansion of aggressive nationalist states and the outbreak of World War II
choices.D: The disappearance of state propaganda

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of aggressive nationalist states and the outbreak of World War II
questions.explanation: Fascist and authoritarian movements contributed to militarism, expansionism, and World War II.

questions.prompt: Originalized policy speech, Tanzania, late 1960s: “Independence must reach the village. We shall build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms so that development is not owned by foreign firms or a few city men. Our socialism is to make citizens, not subjects.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To promote a postcolonial development program based on rural cooperation and state planning
choices.B: To restore direct European colonial rule
choices.C: To reject all government involvement in economic life
choices.D: To demand annexation by a Cold War superpower

questions.correctAnswerText: To promote a postcolonial development program based on rural cooperation and state planning
questions.explanation: The speech calls for schools, clinics, cooperative farms, and national development after independence.

questions.prompt: Originalized policy speech, Tanzania, late 1960s: “Independence must reach the village. We shall build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms so that development is not owned by foreign firms or a few city men. Our socialism is to make citizens, not subjects.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the speaker linked independence to social transformation?

choices.A: “Foreign firms”
choices.B: “A few city men”
choices.C: “Build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms”
choices.D: “Late 1960s”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows the speaker’s focus on transforming village life after independence.

questions.prompt: Originalized policy speech, Tanzania, late 1960s: “Independence must reach the village. We shall build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms so that development is not owned by foreign firms or a few city men. Our socialism is to make citizens, not subjects.” The speech reflects which broader postcolonial challenge?

choices.A: The return of plantation slavery in Africa
choices.B: The disappearance of rural poverty after independence
choices.C: The complete rejection of education as a development goal
choices.D: The effort to create economic development while reducing foreign dependency

questions.correctAnswerText: The effort to create economic development while reducing foreign dependency
questions.explanation: The speech rejects foreign ownership and emphasizes cooperative development.

questions.prompt: Originalized policy speech, Tanzania, late 1960s: “Independence must reach the village. We shall build schools, clinics, and cooperative farms so that development is not owned by foreign firms or a few city men. Our socialism is to make citizens, not subjects.” Which statement best evaluates the speaker’s argument?

choices.A: The speaker argues that independence should remain symbolic rather than social.
choices.B: The speaker defines true independence as political freedom combined with social services, rural development, and economic control.
choices.C: The speaker claims that foreign corporations should direct village life.
choices.D: The speaker rejects nationalism in favor of imperial citizenship.

questions.correctAnswerText: The speaker defines true independence as political freedom combined with social services, rural development, and economic control.
questions.explanation: The speaker links independence to villages, schools, clinics, cooperatives, and reduced foreign dependence.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people, approximate regional averages
Region | 2000 | 2020
Sub-Saharan Africa | 2 | 82
South Asia | 1 | 86
Europe | 55 | 121
Latin America | 12 | 104
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Mobile communication expanded globally, but regions began from unequal levels of access.
choices.B: Mobile technology disappeared in industrialized regions after 2000.
choices.C: All regions had identical access to mobile phones in 2000.
choices.D: Globalization eliminated every regional difference by 2020.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mobile communication expanded globally, but regions began from unequal levels of access.
questions.explanation: The table shows growth in all regions, but starting points in 2000 differed greatly.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people, approximate regional averages
Region | 2000 | 2020
Sub-Saharan Africa | 2 | 82
South Asia | 1 | 86
Europe | 55 | 121
Latin America | 12 | 104
The trend shown in the table most directly contributed to which development?

choices.A: The end of international finance
choices.B: The decline of all long-distance communication
choices.C: The disappearance of digital markets
choices.D: The expansion of mobile banking, online commerce, and real-time communication

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of mobile banking, online commerce, and real-time communication
questions.explanation: Expanding mobile access supported digital economic activity and communication.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people, approximate regional averages
Region | 2000 | 2020
Sub-Saharan Africa | 2 | 82
South Asia | 1 | 86
Europe | 55 | 121
Latin America | 12 | 104
Which evidence best supports the claim that access widened dramatically outside Europe?

choices.A: Sub-Saharan Africa increased from 2 to 82, and South Asia increased from 1 to 86.
choices.B: Europe had 55 subscriptions per 100 people in 2000.
choices.C: Latin America had 12 subscriptions per 100 people in 2000.
choices.D: Europe reached 121 subscriptions per 100 people by 2020.

questions.correctAnswerText: Sub-Saharan Africa increased from 2 to 82, and South Asia increased from 1 to 86.
questions.explanation: These figures show major expansion in regions that had very low access in 2000.

questions.prompt:
Table: Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people, approximate regional averages
Region | 2000 | 2020
Sub-Saharan Africa | 2 | 82
South Asia | 1 | 86
Europe | 55 | 121
Latin America | 12 | 104
Which cultural development was most directly facilitated by the trend shown?

choices.A: The end of popular culture across borders
choices.B: The disappearance of youth activism
choices.C: The rapid circulation of music, images, political messages, and social media content
choices.D: The restoration of isolated local information systems

questions.correctAnswerText: The rapid circulation of music, images, political messages, and social media content
questions.explanation: Mobile connectivity facilitated rapid global cultural exchange and digital communication.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of northern Eurasia c. 1650 shows Russian forts spreading eastward along major rivers into Siberia. Arrows mark the collection of fur tribute from forest peoples and the movement of sable pelts westward toward Moscow.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Russian expansion was limited to overseas colonies in the Atlantic.
choices.B: Siberian expansion was unrelated to environmental resources.
choices.C: Russian state expansion used river routes and fur resources to extend control eastward.
choices.D: Forest peoples controlled Moscow’s central bureaucracy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Russian state expansion used river routes and fur resources to extend control eastward.
questions.explanation: The map shows forts, river routes, fur tribute, and movement of pelts toward Moscow.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of northern Eurasia c. 1650 shows Russian forts spreading eastward along major rivers into Siberia. Arrows mark the collection of fur tribute from forest peoples and the movement of sable pelts westward toward Moscow.” If the map were produced by a Russian official, which point of view would most likely shape it?

choices.A: An emphasis on forts, tribute collection, and the orderly extension of state authority
choices.B: A focus on the cultural autonomy of every Siberian community
choices.C: A rejection of resource extraction as a motive for expansion
choices.D: An argument that Russia had no interest in overland empire building

questions.correctAnswerText: An emphasis on forts, tribute collection, and the orderly extension of state authority
questions.explanation: A Russian official would likely frame expansion as orderly state-building and tribute collection.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of northern Eurasia c. 1650 shows Russian forts spreading eastward along major rivers into Siberia. Arrows mark the collection of fur tribute from forest peoples and the movement of sable pelts westward toward Moscow.” Which map feature most directly supports the claim that economic motives shaped Russian expansion?

choices.A: The location of northern forests
choices.B: The presence of major rivers
choices.C: The eastward spread of forts
choices.D: The movement of sable pelts westward toward Moscow

questions.correctAnswerText: The movement of sable pelts westward toward Moscow
questions.explanation: The movement of furs shows the economic motive behind expansion.

questions.prompt: Originalized workers’ petition, industrial city in western Europe, c. 1848: “The machines do not tire, but the children do. The owners speak of liberty in contracts, yet hunger leaves little liberty to refuse. We ask for shorter hours, inspection of factories, and the right to organize.” Which audience was the petition most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Plantation overseers in the Caribbean
choices.B: Urban reformers, legislators, and workers debating industrial labor conditions
choices.C: Nomadic confederations in Central Asia
choices.D: Missionaries seeking to regulate overseas trade

questions.correctAnswerText: Urban reformers, legislators, and workers debating industrial labor conditions
questions.explanation: The petition asks for labor regulation and organizing rights, making reformers, lawmakers, and workers the likely audience.

questions.prompt: Originalized workers’ petition, industrial city in western Europe, c. 1848: “The machines do not tire, but the children do. The owners speak of liberty in contracts, yet hunger leaves little liberty to refuse. We ask for shorter hours, inspection of factories, and the right to organize.” Which broader context best explains the concerns expressed in the petition?

choices.A: The growth of factory labor and new social criticism during industrialization
choices.B: The decline of urban populations during industrialization
choices.C: The end of wage labor in nineteenth-century Europe
choices.D: The disappearance of child labor before the spread of factories

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of factory labor and new social criticism during industrialization
questions.explanation: The petition reflects industrial working conditions, child labor, and calls for reform.

questions.prompt: Originalized workers’ petition, industrial city in western Europe, c. 1848: “The machines do not tire, but the children do. The owners speak of liberty in contracts, yet hunger leaves little liberty to refuse. We ask for shorter hours, inspection of factories, and the right to organize.” The petition most directly reflects the rise of which ideology or movement?

choices.A: Mercantilism
choices.B: Absolutism
choices.C: Labor socialism and reform movements
choices.D: Manorialism

questions.correctAnswerText: Labor socialism and reform movements
questions.explanation: The petition criticizes unequal labor contracts and calls for collective organization and factory regulation.

questions.prompt: Originalized workers’ petition, industrial city in western Europe, c. 1848: “The machines do not tire, but the children do. The owners speak of liberty in contracts, yet hunger leaves little liberty to refuse. We ask for shorter hours, inspection of factories, and the right to organize.” Which statement best evaluates the petition’s argument?

choices.A: It defends laissez-faire contracts as fully equal for workers and owners.
choices.B: It argues that industrial workers should return to serfdom.
choices.C: It rejects state regulation of labor.
choices.D: It challenges the idea that formal contractual freedom is meaningful under conditions of poverty and unequal power.

questions.correctAnswerText: It challenges the idea that formal contractual freedom is meaningful under conditions of poverty and unequal power.
questions.explanation: The petition argues that hunger limits workers’ ability to refuse unfair labor conditions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of New Zealand, c. 1860–1900, shows expanding settler farms, ports connected by steamship routes, and rail lines carrying wool and frozen meat to export harbors. Shaded areas mark Māori land losses near major settlement zones.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Settler colonial expansion reshaped landholding and connected pastoral production to global markets.
choices.B: Māori communities gained complete control over settler ports.
choices.C: Industrial-era transportation had no effect on export agriculture.
choices.D: New Zealand remained isolated from global trade after 1860.

questions.correctAnswerText: Settler colonial expansion reshaped landholding and connected pastoral production to global markets.
questions.explanation: The map links settler farms, Māori land losses, railways, ports, and exports.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of New Zealand, c. 1860–1900, shows expanding settler farms, ports connected by steamship routes, and rail lines carrying wool and frozen meat to export harbors. Shaded areas mark Māori land losses near major settlement zones.” Which technological development most directly supported the export pattern described?

choices.A: Movable-type printing
choices.B: Refrigerated shipping and steam transportation
choices.C: Gunpowder artillery in land-based empires
choices.D: The compass in medieval caravan trade

questions.correctAnswerText: Refrigerated shipping and steam transportation
questions.explanation: Refrigeration and steam transport enabled distant export of meat and wool through global markets.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of New Zealand, c. 1860–1900, shows expanding settler farms, ports connected by steamship routes, and rail lines carrying wool and frozen meat to export harbors. Shaded areas mark Māori land losses near major settlement zones.” Which visual feature most directly shows the social impact of settler colonialism?

choices.A: The steamship routes
choices.B: The export harbors
choices.C: The rail lines
choices.D: The shaded areas marking Māori land losses

questions.correctAnswerText: The shaded areas marking Māori land losses
questions.explanation: The shaded areas directly indicate Indigenous land loss caused by settler expansion.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of New Zealand, c. 1860–1900, shows expanding settler farms, ports connected by steamship routes, and rail lines carrying wool and frozen meat to export harbors. Shaded areas mark Māori land losses near major settlement zones.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Imperial economies could combine settler land acquisition, transportation infrastructure, and export agriculture.
choices.B: Industrialization ended settler colonialism in Oceania.
choices.C: Export agriculture declined as railroads expanded.
choices.D: Indigenous landholding expanded wherever global markets grew.

questions.correctAnswerText: Imperial economies could combine settler land acquisition, transportation infrastructure, and export agriculture.
questions.explanation: The map shows land acquisition, rail and port infrastructure, and export-oriented pastoral production.

questions.prompt:
Table: Foreign trade at Guangzhou under Qing regulation, simplified pattern
Goods entering China | Goods leaving China | Rule noted by officials
Silver, clocks, woolens | Tea, silk, porcelain | Foreign merchants restricted to licensed trade seasons and approved intermediaries
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Qing officials attempted to regulate foreign trade while allowing selected commercial exchange.
choices.B: China banned all maritime commerce after 1450.
choices.C: European merchants controlled the entire Qing bureaucracy.
choices.D: Tea and porcelain had no role in global trade.

questions.correctAnswerText: Qing officials attempted to regulate foreign trade while allowing selected commercial exchange.
questions.explanation: The table shows foreign goods entering, Chinese goods leaving, and official restrictions on merchants.

questions.prompt:
Table: Foreign trade at Guangzhou under Qing regulation, simplified pattern
Goods entering China | Goods leaving China | Rule noted by officials
Silver, clocks, woolens | Tea, silk, porcelain | Foreign merchants restricted to licensed trade seasons and approved intermediaries
Which purpose would Qing officials most likely have had in creating the rules described?

choices.A: To encourage foreign merchants to govern Chinese provinces
choices.B: To increase state supervision over foreign commercial activity
choices.C: To abolish the production of luxury goods
choices.D: To replace maritime commerce with nomadic pastoralism

questions.correctAnswerText: To increase state supervision over foreign commercial activity
questions.explanation: Licensed seasons and approved intermediaries show Qing efforts to supervise foreign trade.

questions.prompt:
Table: Foreign trade at Guangzhou under Qing regulation, simplified pattern
Goods entering China | Goods leaving China | Rule noted by officials
Silver, clocks, woolens | Tea, silk, porcelain | Foreign merchants restricted to licensed trade seasons and approved intermediaries
The goods leaving China in the table are best understood as part of which broader process?

choices.A: The global demand for Asian luxury and consumer goods
choices.B: The decline of all Chinese manufacturing
choices.C: The replacement of trade by missionary activity
choices.D: The isolation of East Asia from oceanic commerce

questions.correctAnswerText: The global demand for Asian luxury and consumer goods
questions.explanation: Tea, silk, and porcelain were important Asian goods in early modern global commerce.

questions.prompt:
Table: Foreign trade at Guangzhou under Qing regulation, simplified pattern
Goods entering China | Goods leaving China | Rule noted by officials
Silver, clocks, woolens | Tea, silk, porcelain | Foreign merchants restricted to licensed trade seasons and approved intermediaries
Which earlier pattern of Afro-Eurasian exchange most closely resembles the demand for Chinese luxury goods shown in the table?

choices.A: The end of urban markets in Song China
choices.B: The isolation of East Asia from long-distance routes
choices.C: The movement of silk, porcelain, and other luxury goods through Silk Road and maritime networks
choices.D: The disappearance of merchant diasporas before 1450

questions.correctAnswerText: The movement of silk, porcelain, and other luxury goods through Silk Road and maritime networks
questions.explanation: Earlier Afro-Eurasian networks also carried Chinese luxury goods over land and sea.

questions.prompt:
Timeline: International and transregional claims to authority
c. 1250 — A ruler in Dar al-Islam sponsors judges, schools, and charitable foundations to display piety and justice.
1919 — International officials debate mandates over former empires after World War I.
1945 — The United Nations Charter refers to equal rights and self-determination.
1970s — Human rights organizations publicize abuses across national borders.
Which broader context best explains the c. 1250 entry?

choices.A: The decline of Islamic legal and educational institutions
choices.B: The spread of industrial capitalism
choices.C: The role of Islamic institutions in legitimizing rule and organizing urban society
choices.D: The creation of modern international law after World War II

questions.correctAnswerText: The role of Islamic institutions in legitimizing rule and organizing urban society
questions.explanation: Sponsoring judges, schools, and charitable foundations reflected Islamic political and social legitimacy.

questions.prompt:
Timeline: International and transregional claims to authority
c. 1250 — A ruler in Dar al-Islam sponsors judges, schools, and charitable foundations to display piety and justice.
1919 — International officials debate mandates over former empires after World War I.
1945 — The United Nations Charter refers to equal rights and self-determination.
1970s — Human rights organizations publicize abuses across national borders.
Which purpose most directly shaped the mandate debates after World War I?

choices.A: To manage former imperial territories while claiming international oversight and eventual development
choices.B: To abolish all colonial rule immediately in 1919
choices.C: To restore the Mongol Empire
choices.D: To create a global free-trade zone without political supervision

questions.correctAnswerText: To manage former imperial territories while claiming international oversight and eventual development
questions.explanation: Mandates were justified as international oversight of former imperial territories after World War I.

questions.prompt:
Timeline: International and transregional claims to authority
c. 1250 — A ruler in Dar al-Islam sponsors judges, schools, and charitable foundations to display piety and justice.
1919 — International officials debate mandates over former empires after World War I.
1945 — The United Nations Charter refers to equal rights and self-determination.
1970s — Human rights organizations publicize abuses across national borders.
The 1945 reference to self-determination most directly contributed to which postwar development?

choices.A: The end of all nationalist movements
choices.B: The growth of anticolonial claims for independence in Asia and Africa
choices.C: The restoration of European empires without criticism
choices.D: The disappearance of international organizations

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of anticolonial claims for independence in Asia and Africa
questions.explanation: Self-determination language strengthened postwar independence claims.

questions.prompt:
Timeline: International and transregional claims to authority
c. 1250 — A ruler in Dar al-Islam sponsors judges, schools, and charitable foundations to display piety and justice.
1919 — International officials debate mandates over former empires after World War I.
1945 — The United Nations Charter refers to equal rights and self-determination.
1970s — Human rights organizations publicize abuses across national borders.
Which change over time is best shown by the timeline?

choices.A: Claims to legitimate authority became less connected to public values after 1900.
choices.B: Religious authority disappeared completely after 1250.
choices.C: International organizations eliminated all conflict after 1945.
choices.D: Ideas about justice and legitimacy increasingly operated through global institutions and transnational activism.

questions.correctAnswerText: Ideas about justice and legitimacy increasingly operated through global institutions and transnational activism.
questions.explanation: The timeline moves from religiously grounded rule to international organizations and global human-rights activism.
